official_name: National Institute of Standards and Technology
notes: NIST produces authoritative security control frameworks used globally as references for cybersecurity requirements. Federal agency with regulatory influence, research-backed, referenced in executive orders and regulations, used internationally. Publishes SP 800-53 (mandatory for US federal systems) and the Cybersecurity Framework (voluntary risk management).

urls.note: Computer Security Resource Center — primary home for NIST cybersecurity publications

match_nodes.description: NIST SP 800-53 Security and Privacy Controls

data.official_name: NIST Special Publication 800-53: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations
data.description: Federal security and privacy controls. Mandatory for US federal systems, widely adopted as reference baseline globally.
data.notes:
Rev 5 (with Update 1) is current. 20 control families identified by two-letter codes (AC, AT, AU, etc.). Controls are numbered within families (AC-1, AC-2, etc.). Control enhancements use parenthesized numbers: AC-2(1), SI-7(6). Note: the parentheses are part of the identifier — preserve them exactly.

No stable official per-control web URLs exist. The NIST CPRT (Cybersecurity and Privacy Reference Tool) at csrc.nist.gov provides interactive browsing but uses JavaScript SPA hash fragments, not stable linkable URLs. Machine-readable data available in OSCAL format on GitHub.

Third-party per-control URLs available at csf.tools (e.g., https://csf.tools/reference/nist-sp-800-53/r5/ac/ac-1/). Note: csf.tools requires lowercased control family and ID in the URL path.

Basis for FedRAMP, FISMA, and many other compliance frameworks. Rev 5 added privacy controls (PT family) and supply chain risk management controls (SR family).

versions_available.note: Rev 5 with Update 1 (Dec 2024). 20 control families, over 1,000 controls and enhancements. Added PT and SR families.
18 control families. Still referenced in older compliance documentation.

urls.note: Official publication page for Rev 5 Update 1
NIST CPRT interactive browser (JavaScript SPA — not directly linkable per control)
OSCAL (Open Security Controls Assessment Language) machine-readable format. Preferred for programmatic access.
Third-party per-control browser (csf.tools). Not operated by NIST.

children.description: Control family (e.g., AC, SI, PT). Contains multiple controls.

data.note: No per-family web page. Return the CPRT or csf.tools URL from the parent with instructions to filter by family code.

examples.note: Access Control family — the most commonly referenced 800-53 family

children.description: Base control (e.g., AC-1, SI-7). Belongs to a family.

data.note: No official per-control web URL. To find this control: (1) Use the OSCAL data from GitHub for programmatic access. (2) Browse the NIST CPRT interactive tool. (3) Third-party: csf.tools provides per-control pages (requires lowercased family and control ID in URL path).

children.description: Control enhancement (e.g., AC-2(1), SI-7(6)). Parentheses are part of the ID — preserve them exactly.

data.note: Control enhancements add specificity to base controls. Same resolution approach as base controls — no official per-enhancement URL. The parenthesized number is part of the source identifier format.

examples.note: Parentheses are part of the identifier — preserve exactly. No official per-enhancement URL.

data.description: Voluntary framework for managing and reducing cybersecurity risk, organized by functions, categories, and subcategories.
data.notes:
CSF 2.0 (released February 2024) is current. Organized hierarchically: 6 functions → 22 categories → 106 subcategories.

CSF 2.0 added the Govern (GV) function — CSF 1.1 had only 5 functions (ID, PR, DE, RS, RC). The ID numbering changed between versions, so version matters for correct resolution.

Subcategory IDs use the format Function.Category-NN (e.g., GV.RM-01, ID.AM-01, PR.AC-01). Function codes are two uppercase letters. Category codes are two uppercase letters prefixed by their function.

No stable official per-subcategory web URLs exist. Same situation as 800-53 — the NIST site uses JavaScript SPA navigation. Third-party per-subcategory URLs available at csf.tools.

Maps to other frameworks including ISO 27001, CIS Controls, and NIST SP 800-53. Widely adopted in private sector and referenced in regulations. Includes implementation tiers (Partial, Risk Informed, Repeatable, Adaptive).

versions_available.note: 6 functions (added Govern), 22 categories, 106 subcategories. Major restructuring from 1.1.
5 functions (no Govern), 23 categories, 108 subcategories. Different numbering than 2.0.

urls.note: Third-party per-subcategory browser (csf.tools). Not operated by NIST.

children.description: CSF function (e.g., GV, ID, PR). Top-level organizing concept.

data.note: Functions are the highest-level organizing concepts in the CSF. CSF 2.0 has 6 functions; CSF 1.1 had 5 (no Govern).

examples.note: Govern — new in CSF 2.0 (not present in 1.1)

children.description: CSF category (e.g., GV.RM, ID.AM, PR.AC). Groups related subcategories.

data.note: Categories group related subcategories under a function. CSF 2.0 has 22 categories across 6 functions. No per-category web page; return the CSF reference tool URL with instructions to filter by category.

examples.note: Risk Management Strategy — under Govern function

known_values.PR.AA: Identity Management, Authentication, and Access Control

children.description: CSF subcategory (e.g., GV.RM-01, ID.AM-01). The specific control outcome.

data.note: Subcategories are the specific control outcomes in the CSF. No official per-subcategory web URL. To find this subcategory: (1) Use the OSCAL data from GitHub for programmatic access. (2) Browse the NIST CSF Reference Tool. (3) Third-party: csf.tools provides per-subcategory pages.
